description:
You are acting as a DevOps engineer who has just installed a lightweight, fictional binary-repository service called “artie-manager” in your home directory.  
  The basic directory tree now looks like this:

      /home/user/artie-manager/
          └── config/
              └── artie.conf      (currently empty)

  Goal  
  ----  
  Configure “artie-manager” so that it will:  
  1. listen on TCP port 8081,  
  2. host two repositories named “releases” and “snapshots”, and  
  3. store all repository data under /home/user/artie-manager/repos/.

  What you have to do  
  -------------------  
  a. Edit /home/user/artie-manager/config/artie.conf so it contains **exactly** the following three key=value lines (no extra whitespace before or after the keys, and one final trailing newline at the end of the file):

      port=8081
      repos=releases,snapshots
      storage=/home/user/artie-manager/repos

  b. Create the directory structure for the repositories so that, *after you are done*, both of these directories exist and are empty:

      /home/user/artie-manager/repos/releases
      /home/user/artie-manager/repos/snapshots

  c. Produce a short verification log named /home/user/artie-manager/setup.log whose contents **must match this template character-for-character** (each line ends with a Unix newline `\n`; the order is important, and there is **no** blank line at the end):

      PORT: 8081
      REPO_CREATED: releases=/home/user/artie-manager/repos/releases
      REPO_CREATED: snapshots=/home/user/artie-manager/repos/snapshots

  d. Make sure all three files/directories (the edited artie.conf, the new repo directories, and setup.log) are world-readable but not world-writable (i.e., permissions 755 for directories and 644 for files are acceptable).

  Constraints & tips  
  ------------------  
  • You do **not** need to actually start a service; only prepare the configuration and log.  
  • Use only standard GNU/Linux utilities available in a minimal container (mkdir, echo, printf, chmod, etc.).  
  • You should need no more than 3–4 commands in total if you combine actions efficiently.  
  • The automated grader will fail if:  
      – artie.conf does not contain the exact three lines shown (including correct order and newline handling).  
      – either repository directory is missing or misspelled.  
      – setup.log deviates from the required text or ordering in any way.  
      – permissions are too permissive (world-writable) or too restrictive (owner can’t read).  

  Proceed by opening a terminal and performing the required steps. When everything is set correctly, you’re done.
truth:
The automated test will assert:

  1. File /home/user/artie-manager/config/artie.conf exists and its **exact** byte content is:  
     "port=8081\nrepos=releases,snapshots\nstorage=/home/user/artie-manager/repos\n"

  2. Directories  
        /home/user/artie-manager/repos/releases  
        /home/user/artie-manager/repos/snapshots  
     both exist and are directories (not files, symlinks, etc.).

  3. File /home/user/artie-manager/setup.log exists and its **exact** byte content is:  
     "PORT: 8081\nREPO_CREATED: releases=/home/user/artie-manager/repos/releases\nREPO_CREATED: snapshots=/home/user/artie-manager/repos/snapshots\n"

  4. Permissions:  
     • artie.conf and setup.log have mode 0644 or stricter (i.e., rw-r--r-- or similar).  
     • repos/, releases/, and snapshots/ directories have mode 0755 or stricter (rwxr-xr-x or similar).  
     • Nothing under /home/user/artie-manager/ is world-writable.

  No additional files (other than default “.” and “..”) may exist inside /home/user/artie-manager/repos/releases or /home/user/artie-manager/repos/snapshots.
